Oct 12, 2025 Â· Paul Kronlund-Drouault  Â· 9 min read

A quick overview of RegEx

Regular Expressions (RegEx) are a very common tool in computer science, often used for search-and-replace operations, finding specific strings or patterns in large files, and validating user input (see the infamous email regex).

In introductory classes to theoretical computer science, we see regular expressions as an example application of automata theory, more precisely finite automata. The process of converting a RegEx into an automaton is called Thompsonâs construction, and is behind grepâs unreal speed.

The problem at hand

When building Proposition 7, I was struggling with a very peculiar problem in program synthesis and input validation.

Correctness by complete-ability

The p7 expression parser is based on a very simple principle:

With Î£\SigmaÎ£ an alphabet and LLL a language, we can define a function

Î¦:Î£ââ{accept,reject}\Phi : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \{\text{accept},\text{reject}\}Î¦:Î£ââ{accept,reject}

as follows:

Î¦(s)={acceptâsâ²âÎ£â,âssâ²âLrejectotherwise\Phi(s) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \exists s' \in \Sigma^*,\, ss' \in L \\ \text{reject} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}Î¦(s)={acceptrejectââsâ²âÎ£â,ssâ²âLotherwiseâ

Our goal here, for constrained generation, is to ensure that any prefix sss of a valid expression can be completed into a valid expression in LLL and thus lead to a valid program of proof.

RegEx sub-problem

Since our language LLL is defined by complex mechanisms, such as typing rules and recursive grammars, it must be handled by a Turing-complete model in the general case. However, a subset of this problem requires a generalized solution for RegEx.

Consider the following grammar as an example:

expr ::= /b(u|o|i)p/
term ::= expr ('and' expr)* 
prog ::= term 'at' 'bap'

expressions between slashes (/<regex>/) are regexes

The program bip at bap is a trivial valid expression, as is bip and bop at bap. However, bip and is not a valid expression, but can be completed into one by adding another expr + at bap after the and. We can call it Î£-completeable\Sigma\text{-completeable}Î£-completeable in LLL.

Now, consider that we are working with a system that produces output byte by byte. How can we validate the expression bi or bip an? Both are prefixes of completeable expressions, but neither is a Î£-completeable\Sigma\text{-completeable}Î£-completeable expression.

Formally, we could say that our grammar defines a language LLL over an alphabet Î£={/b(u|o|i)p/,âandâ,â(â,â)â,âatâ,âbapâ}\Sigma = \{ \text{/b(u|o|i)p/}, \text{'and'}, \text{'('}, \text{')'}, \text{'at'}, \text{'bap'} \}Î£={/b(u|o|i)p/,âandâ,â(â,â)â,âatâ,âbapâ} and that this alphabet is itself a language LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â over the alphabet of bytes Î£byte=[0..255]\Sigma_{\text{byte}} = [0..255]Î£byteâ=[0..255] (or more generally Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ corresponding to any vocabulary where Î£vocabâÎ£byteâ\Sigma_\text{vocab} \subseteq \Sigma_{\text{byte}}^*Î£vocabââÎ£byteââ and Î£âÎ£vocabâ\Sigma \subseteq \Sigma_\text{vocab}^*Î£âÎ£vocabââ).

Disambiguation of LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â

LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â can be decomposed as the union of all keywords in Î£\SigmaÎ£. Each element lâÎ£l \in \SigmalâÎ£ defines a language LlâÎ£vocabâL_l \subseteq \Sigma_\text{vocab}^*LlââÎ£vocabââ, where LlL_lLlâ is either a singleton set (for fixed keywords) or a regular language (for patterns like identifiers)1:

LÎ£=âlâÎ£LlL_\Sigma = \bigcup_{l\in \Sigma} L_lLÎ£â=lâÎ£ââLlâ

For example, in our grammar:

  • Lâandâ={âandâ}L_{\text{'and'}} = \{\text{'and'}\}Lâandââ={âandâ} (a fixed keyword)
  • L/b(u|o|i)p/={âbupâ,âbopâ,âbipâ}L_{\text{/b(u|o|i)p/}} = \{\text{'bup'}, \text{'bop'}, \text{'bip'}\}L/b(u|o|i)p/â={âbupâ,âbopâ,âbipâ} (a regex pattern)

Each LlL_lLlâ can be represented by a regular expression rlr_lrlâ such that L(rl)=LlL(r_l) = L_lL(rlâ)=Llâ. Therefore:

LÎ£=âlâÎ£L(rl)L_\Sigma = \bigcup_{l\in \Sigma} L(r_l)LÎ£â=lâÎ£ââL(rlâ)

Following this, we can define rÎ£=âlâÎ£rlr_\Sigma = \bigcup_{l \in \Sigma} r_lrÎ£â=âlâÎ£ârlâ such that L(rÎ£)=LÎ£L(r_\Sigma) = L_\SigmaL(rÎ£â)=LÎ£â

Validation from Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ to LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â

To validate partial inputs over Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ, we can define a function

Ï(s):Î£vocabââ{complete,partial,reject}\pi(s) : \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^* \rightarrow \{\text{complete},\text{partial},\text{reject}\}Ï(s):Î£vocabâââ{complete,partial,reject}

as follows:

Ï(s)={completesâLÎ£partialsâLÎ£â§âsâ²âÎ£vocabâ,âssâ²âLÎ£rejectotherwise\pi(s) = \begin{cases} \text{complete} & s \in L_\Sigma \\ \text{partial} & s \notin L_\Sigma \land \exists s' \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*,\, ss' \in L_\Sigma \\ \text{reject} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}Ï(s)=â©â¨â§âcompletepartialrejectâsâLÎ£âsâ/LÎ£ââ§âsâ²âÎ£vocabââ,ssâ²âLÎ£âotherwiseâ

This function bridges the gap between the formal languageâs alphabet and the model vocabulary, allowing us to validate all possible partial (complete-able) inputs.

Î£vocab-completeability\Sigma_{\text{vocab}}\text{-completeability}Î£vocabâ-completeability in LLL

Now that we have a function relating our modelâs vocabulary Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ to the formal languageâs alphabet Î£\SigmaÎ£, we can define a function that directly relates the modelâs vocabulary to the completeability in LLL noted Î¦^\hat{\Phi}Î¦^:

Î¦^:Î£vocabââ{accept,reject}\hat{\Phi}: \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^* \rightarrow \{\text{accept},\text{reject}\}Î¦^:Î£vocabâââ{accept,reject}

Let t1â¯tnâtâ²t_1 \cdots t_n \cdot t't1ââ¯tnââtâ² be a tokenization of input sâÎ£vocabâs \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*sâÎ£vocabââ where t1,â¦,tnâÎ£t_1, \ldots, t_n \in \Sigmat1â,â¦,tnââÎ£ are complete tokens and tâ²âÎ£vocabât' \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*tâ²âÎ£vocabââ is the remaining partial input (noted Îµ\varepsilonÎµ if empty).

Î¦^(s)={accepttâ²=Îµâ§Î¦(t1â¯tn)=acceptaccepttâ²â Îµâ§Ï(tâ²)=partialâ§(âqâÎ£vocabâ,tâ²qâLÎ£,Î¦(t1â¯tn(tâ²q))=accept)rejectotherwise\hat{\Phi}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & t' = \varepsilon \land \Phi(t_1 \cdots t_n) = \text{accept} \\ \text{accept} & t' \neq \varepsilon \land \pi(t') = \text{partial} \land (\exists q \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*, t'q \in L_\Sigma, \Phi(t_1 \cdots t_{n}(t' q)) = \text{accept})\\ \text{reject} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}Î¦^(s)=â©â¨â§âacceptacceptrejectâtâ²=Îµâ§Î¦(t1ââ¯tnâ)=accepttâ²î =Îµâ§Ï(tâ²)=partialâ§(âqâÎ£vocabââ,tâ²qâLÎ£â,Î¦(t1ââ¯tnâ(tâ²q))=accept)otherwiseâ

For disambiguation see2

This captures that we accept a partial input if either

  • It fully tokenizes into element of Î£\SigmaÎ£ and parses correctly in LLL
  • The partial token tâ²t'tâ² can be completed by some qâÎ£vocabâq \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*qâÎ£vocabââ into a valid token lâÎ£l \in \SigmalâÎ£ such that and appending lll to the complete tokens yields an expression that is Î£-completeable\Sigma\text{-completeable}Î£-completeable in LLL.

Keeping with our example, bi and bip an are both Î£vocab-completeable\Sigma_{\text{vocab}}\text{-completeable}Î£vocabâ-completeable in LLL, as they can be completed into valid expressions in LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â: bip and bip and that are themselves Î£-completeable\Sigma\text{-completeable}Î£-completeable in LLL. On the other hand, bip at bo is not Î£vocab-completeable\Sigma_{\text{vocab}}\text{-completeable}Î£vocabâ-completeable in LLL, because while it can be completed into the LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â expression bip at bop, this expression is not Î£-completeable\Sigma\text{-completeable}Î£-completeable in LLL (it is even outright invalid).

Implementation

Focusing on the regex aspect of the program, we want to implement Ï(s)\pi(s)Ï(s) for the case where LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â is defined by a regex rÎ£r_\SigmarÎ£â. We cannot trivially do this by directly manipulating automata, but need a more general mechanism on the expressions themselves. In short, we need something that can tell us if a string sâÎ£vocabâs \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*sâÎ£vocabââ is a valid prefix of a string in LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â.

One approach is find the set of suffixes S={sâ²âÎ£vocabââ£ssâ²âLÎ£}S = \{ s' \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^* \mid ss' \in L_\Sigma \}S={sâ²âÎ£vocabâââ£ssâ²âLÎ£â}, and check if SSS is non-empty. The completing suffix of sss is known as the quotient of LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â with respect to sss, and is written sâ1LÎ£s^{-1}L_\Sigmasâ1LÎ£â. The expression that can produce this set of quotients is known as the Brzozowski derivative of LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â with respect to sss, and is written Ds(LÎ£)D_s(L_\Sigma)Dsâ(LÎ£â).

We work with a standard regular-expression syntax over the alphabet Î£vocab\Sigma_{\text{vocab}}Î£vocabâ to define LÎ£L_\SigmaLÎ£â:

  • â\emptysetâ (empty language), Îµ\varepsilonÎµ (empty word)
  • literals aâÎ£vocaba \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}aâÎ£vocabâ and character ranges [a..b]={xâÎ£vocabâ£aâ¤xâ¤b}[a..b] = \{ x \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}} \mid a \le x \le b \}[a..b]={xâÎ£vocabââ£aâ¤xâ¤b}
  • union r+sr + sr+s, concatenation rsrsrs and Kleene star râr^*râ

Let L(r)âÎ£vocabâL(r) \subseteq \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*L(r)âÎ£vocabââ denote the language of regex rrr, so that LÎ£=L(rÎ£)L_\Sigma = L(r_\Sigma)LÎ£â=L(rÎ£â) for some regex rÎ£r_\SigmarÎ£â. The (left) quotient of a language by a character câÎ£vocabc \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}câÎ£vocabâ is

câ1Lââ=ââ{wâÎ£vocabââ£cwâL}.c^{-1}L \;=\; \{ w \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^* \mid cw \in L \}.câ1L={wâÎ£vocabâââ£cwâL}.

We also introduce Î½(r)\nu(r)Î½(r) (ânullableâ) as the predicate that holds exactly when ÎµâL(r)\varepsilon \in L(r)ÎµâL(r):

  • Î½(â)=false\nu(\emptyset) = \text{false}Î½(â)=false
  • Î½(Îµ)=true\nu(\varepsilon) = \text{true}Î½(Îµ)=true
  • Î½(a)=false\nu(a) = \text{false}Î½(a)=false
  • Î½([a..b])=false\nu([a..b]) = \text{false}Î½([a..b])=false
  • Î½(r+s)=Î½(r)â¨Î½(s)\nu(r + s) = \nu(r) \lor \nu(s)Î½(r+s)=Î½(r)â¨Î½(s)
  • Î½(rs)=Î½(r)â§Î½(s)\nu(rs) = \nu(r) \land \nu(s)Î½(rs)=Î½(r)â§Î½(s)
  • Î½(râ)=true\nu(r^*) = \text{true}Î½(râ)=true

We can then define our Brzozowski derivative ( with câÎ£vocabÂ c \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}~câÎ£vocabâÂ ):

Dc(â)=âDc(Îµ)=âDc(a)={ÎµifÂ a=câotherwiseDc([a..b])={ÎµifÂ aâ¤câ¤bâotherwiseDc(r+s)=Dc(r)+Dc(s)Dc(râs)=Dc(r)âsâââªââ{Dc(s)ifÂ Î½(r)âotherwiseDc(râ)=Dc(r)ârâ\begin{aligned} D_c(\emptyset) &= \emptyset \\ D_c(\varepsilon) &= \emptyset \\ D_c(a) &= \begin{cases} \varepsilon & \text{if } a = c \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\ D_c([a..b]) &= \begin{cases} \varepsilon & \text{if } a \le c \le b \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\ D_c(r + s) &= D_c(r) + D_c(s) \\ D_c(r\cdot s) &= D_c(r)\cdot s \;\cup\; \begin{cases} D_c(s) & \text{if } \nu(r) \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\ D_c(r^*) &= D_c(r)\cdot r^* \end{aligned}Dcâ(â)Dcâ(Îµ)Dcâ(a)Dcâ([a..b])Dcâ(r+s)Dcâ(râs)Dcâ(râ)â=â=â={ÎµââifÂ a=cotherwiseâ={ÎµââifÂ aâ¤câ¤botherwiseâ=Dcâ(r)+Dcâ(s)=Dcâ(r)âsâª{Dcâ(s)ââifÂ Î½(r)otherwiseâ=Dcâ(r)ârââ

We can then extend the derivative to arbitrary strings by left-folding on characters (over Î£vocab\Sigma_{\text{vocab}}Î£vocabâ):

DÎµ(r)=r,Dxc(r)=Dc(Dx(r)),xâÎ£vocabâ,ââcâÎ£vocab.\begin{aligned} D_{\varepsilon}(r) &= r, \\ D_{xc}(r) &= D_c\bigl(D_x(r)\bigr), \quad x \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*,\; c \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}. \end{aligned}DÎµâ(r)Dxcâ(r)â=r,=Dcâ(Dxâ(r)),xâÎ£vocabââ,câÎ£vocabâ.â

Equivalently, we apply recursively for s=c1c2â¯cnâÎ£vocabâs = c_1 c_2 \cdots c_n \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*s=c1âc2ââ¯cnââÎ£vocabââ,

Ds(r)=Dcn(â¯Dc2(Dc1(r))â¯â).D_s(r) = D_{c_n}\bigl(\cdots D_{c_2}(D_{c_1}(r))\cdots\bigr).Dsâ(r)=Dcnââ(â¯Dc2ââ(Dc1ââ(r))â¯).

Correctness connects completion to nullability. For the keyword-recognizing regex rÎ£r_\SigmarÎ£â (where L(rÎ£)=LÎ£L(r_\Sigma) = L_\SigmaL(rÎ£â)=LÎ£â):

ssâ²âL(r)âââºââsâ²âL(Dsâ(r)).ss'\in L(r)\iff s'\in L(D_sâ(r)).ssâ²âL(r)âºsâ²âL(Dsââ(r)).

This is precisely the test we need for complete-ability in Ï\piÏ: given a partial input sâÎ£vocabâs \in \Sigma_{\text{vocab}}^*sâÎ£vocabââ, compute Ds(rÎ£)D_s(r_\Sigma)Dsâ(rÎ£â) and check Ds(rÎ£)=âD_s(r_\Sigma) = \emptysetDsâ(rÎ£â)=â:

Ï(s)={completeÎ½(Ds(rÎ£))partialDs(rÎ£)â ârejectotherwise\pi(s) = \begin{cases} \text{complete} & \nu(D_s(r_\Sigma)) \\ \text{partial} & D_s(r_\Sigma) \neq \emptyset \\ \text{reject} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}Ï(s)=â©â¨â§âcompletepartialrejectâÎ½(Dsâ(rÎ£â))Dsâ(rÎ£â)î =âotherwiseâ

cases evaluated in order for disambiguation

Correspondence to code

The Rust definition below implements DcD_cDcâ and then folds it over a string sss:

fn char_derivative(&self, c: char) -> Regex {
    use Regex::*;
    match self {
        Empty => Empty,
        Epsilon => Empty,
        Char(ch) => if *ch == c { Epsilon } else { Empty },
        Range(start, end) => if *start <= c && c <= *end { Epsilon } else { Empty },
        Union(a, b) => Union(Box::new((**a).char_derivative(c)), Box::new((**b).char_derivative(c))),
        Concat(a, b) => {
            let da = a.char_derivative(c);
            if a.nullable() {
                Union(
                    Box::new(Concat(Box::new(da), Box::new((**b).clone()))),
                    Box::new(b.char_derivative(c)),
                )
            } else {
                Concat(Box::new(da), Box::new((**b).clone()))
            }
        }
        Star(r) => Concat(Box::new((**r).char_derivative(c)), Box::new(Star(r.clone()))),
    }
}

pub fn derivative(&self, s: &str) -> Regex {
    s.chars().fold(self.clone(), |acc, c| acc.char_derivative(c))
}

In practice, one also applies algebraic simplifications after each construction (e.g., â+r=r\emptyset + r = râ+r=r, âr=â\emptyset r = \emptysetâr=â, Îµr=r\varepsilon r = rÎµr=r) to control expression growth.

In the real implementation, we use the Brzozowski derivative not only to implement Ï(s)\pi(s)Ï(s), but also as a policy for the sampling system in the constrained decoding core of Proposition 7.

Conclusion

In a real world implementaiton with grammar defined in EBNF-like format and an input sss over Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ, we can implement Î¦^(s)\hat{\Phi}(s)Î¦^(s) as follows:

  1. Tokenize sss into tokens t1,t2,â¦,tnt_1, t_2, \ldots, t_nt1â,t2â,â¦,tnâ over Î£\SigmaÎ£.
  • If tokenization succeeds and sss is fully tokenized, return Î¦(s)\Phi(s)Î¦(s)
  • If tokenization fails with remaning input tâ²t'tâ², continue to step 2
  1. Compute râ²=Dtâ²(rÎ£)r' = D_{t'}(r_\Sigma)râ²=Dtâ²â(rÎ£â) where rÎ£=âlâÎ£rlr_\Sigma = \sum_{l \in \Sigma} r_lrÎ£â=âlâÎ£ârlâ
  2. Build the set Tn+1={yâÎ£â£Â â(tn+2,tn+3,...tn+k)âÎ£â,Î¦(t1t2...tnÂ yÂ tn+2,tn+3,...tn+k)=accept}T_{n+1} = \{y \in \Sigma | ~\exists (t_{n+2},t_{n+3},...t_{n+k}) \in \Sigma^*, \Phi(t_1t_2...t_n~y~t_{n+2},t_{n+3},...t_{n+k}) = \text{accept}\}Tn+1â={yâÎ£â£Â â(tn+2â,tn+3â,...tn+kâ)âÎ£â,Î¦(t1ât2â...tnâÂ yÂ tn+2â,tn+3â,...tn+kâ)=accept} of possible next continuations in LLL
  3. Check if âxâÎ£vocabâ,lâÎ£\exists x \in \Sigma_\text{vocab}^*,l \in \SigmaâxâÎ£vocabââ,lâÎ£ such that âÏâTn+1,Ï=lÂ x\exists \tau \in T_{n+1}, \tau = l~xâÏâTn+1â,Ï=lÂ x where Î½(Dtâ²x(rl))=true\nu(D_{t'x}(r_l)) = \text{true}Î½(Dtâ²xâ(rlâ))=true.
    • If yes, return accept
    • Otherwise, return reject

For the precise use case of constrained generation, Tn+1T_{n+1}Tn+1â and râ²r'râ² are passed to the sampling system and applied as filters on the modelâs vocabulary Î£vocab\Sigma_\text{vocab}Î£vocabâ to only allow tokens that can complete sss into a valid expression.

Obviously, this is only a small piece in the puzzle, and a lot of challenges remain with the implementation of Î¦\PhiÎ¦ on arbitrary, context-dependent, languages, with context and type dependencies. Itâs still cool to see how basic concepts like this have deep implications and practical value in real-world systems.

Footnotes

  1. For the purposes of this article, we assume tokenization follows the longest match principle: when multiple tokens could match a prefix of the input, the longest matching token is selected. This ensures deterministic tokenization and prevents ambiguity in the decomposition above. For instance, if both âaâ and âandâ are in Î£\SigmaÎ£, the input âandâ tokenizes uniquely as the token âandâ rather than âaâ followed by remaining input ândâ. â©

  2. Since Ï(tâ²)=partial\pi(t') = \text{partial}Ï(tâ²)=partial guarantees that some completion of tâ²t'tâ² exists, the second case checks whether at least one such completion (noted here tâ²qt'qtâ²q) validates the Î¦\PhiÎ¦ condition and yields a Î£\SigmaÎ£-completeable expression. For constrained sampling, finding any valid path forward suffices. â©
